name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنشَقَّتْ (١)
```

```english
When the sky is split open (1)
```

```arabic
وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ (٢)
```

```english
and gives ear to its Lord as it should. (2)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ (٣)
```

```english
When the earth is spread out (3)
```

```arabic
وَأَلْقَتْ مَا فِيهَا وَتَخَلَّتْ (٤)
```

```english
and throws out what is in it, emptying itself, (4)
```

```arabic
وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّهَا وَحُقَّتْ (٥)
```

```english
and gives ear to its Lord as it should. (5)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا فَمُلَـٰقِيهِ (٦)
```

```english
O man! You are labouring toward your Lord laboriously, and you will encounter Him. (6)
```

```arabic
فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ بِيَمِينِهِۦ (٧)
```

```english
Then, as for him who is given his record [of deeds] in his right hand, (7)
```

```arabic
فَسَوْفَ يُحَاسَبُ حِسَابًا يَسِيرًا (٨)
```

```english
he will receive an easy reckoning, (8)
```

```arabic
وَيَنقَلِبُ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا (٩)
```

```english
and he will return to his folks joyfully. (9)
```

```arabic
وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِىَ كِتَـٰبَهُۥ وَرَآءَ ظَهْرِهِۦ (١٠)
```

```english
But as for him who is given his record from behind his back, (10)
```

```arabic
فَسَوْفَ يَدْعُوا۟ ثُبُورًا (١١)
```

```english
he will pray for annihilation (11)
```

```arabic
وَيَصْلَىٰ سَعِيرًا (١٢)
```

```english
and enter the Blaze. (12)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ فِىٓ أَهْلِهِۦ مَسْرُورًا (١٣)
```

```english
Indeed he used to be joyful among his folk, (13)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ ظَنَّ أَن لَّن يَحُورَ (١٤)
```

```english
and he thought that he would never return. (14)
```

```arabic
بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ رَبَّهُۥ كَانَ بِهِۦ بَصِيرًا (١٥)
```

```english
Yes, his Lord had been watching him. (15)
```

```arabic
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِٱلشَّفَقِ (١٦)
```

```english
I swear by the evening glow, (16)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّيْلِ وَمَا وَسَقَ (١٧)
```

```english
by the night and what it is fraught with, (17)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْقَمَرِ إِذَا ٱتَّسَقَ (١٨)
```

```english
by the moon when it blooms full: (18)
```

```arabic
لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقًا عَن طَبَقٍ (١٩)
```

```english
you will surely fare from stage to stage. (19)
```

```arabic
فَمَا لَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
What is the matter with them that they will not believe, (20)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْقُرْءَانُ لَا يَسْجُدُونَ ۩ (٢١)
```

```english
and will not prostrate when the Quran is recited to them? (21)
```

```arabic
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يُكَذِّبُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
Indeed, the faithless impugn [the Apostle], (22)
```

```arabic
وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُوعُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
and Allah knows best what they keep to themselves. (23)
```

```arabic
فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٢٤)
```

```english
So inform them of a painful punishment, (24)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍۭ (٢٥)
```

```english
excepting such as are faithful and do righteous deeds: for them there will be an everlasting reward. (25)
```

## (Commentary)

The surah points toward the rising of the Hour (قيام الساعة) and mentions that man is on a journey toward his Lord until he meets Him, whereupon he will be held accountable for what his book necessitates. It emphasizes this discourse, and the tone of warning (الإنذار) prevails over that of glad tidings (التبشير). The context of its verses is that of a Meccan revelation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ...** — "When the sky is split open..." [84:1]. This is a conditional clause whose apodosis is omitted, indicated by His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ...** — "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord..." [84:6]. The implied meaning is: [When the sky is split open...] man shall meet his Lord, who will then reckon with him and requite him for what he has done.

The splitting of the sky (انشقاق السماء)—which is its cracking and opening—is among the portents of the Hour (أشراط الساعة), like the leveling of the earth and other events mentioned in various places of His speech, such as the folding of the sun, the joining of the sun and the moon, the scattering of the stars, and the like.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّها وَحُقَّتْ...** — "and gives ear to its Lord as it should" [84:2]. Giving ear (الإذن) means listening; from it is derived the word for the ear (الأذن) as the organ of hearing. It is a metaphor for submission and obedience. "As it should" (حُقَّتْ) means it was made a reality and worthy of listening. The meaning is: it obeyed and submitted to its Lord, and it was right and fitting for it to listen and obey.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذَا الْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ...** — "and when the earth is leveled" [84:3]. It appears that what is meant is the expansion of the earth. The Exalted has said: **يَوْمَ تُبَدَّلُ الْأَرْضُ غَيْرَ الْأَرْضِ...** — "The day the earth is transformed into another earth..." [14:48].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَلْقَتْ ما فِيها وَتَخَلَّتْ...** — "and throws out what is in it and empties itself" [84:4]. That is, the earth casts out the dead within its belly and goes to great lengths to empty itself of them.

It has been said that it refers to its casting out the dead and the treasures, as the Exalted said: **وَأَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها...** — "and the earth throws out its burdens" [99:2]. It has also been said the meaning is that it casts out what is in its belly and rids itself of what is on its back, such as mountains and seas. Perhaps the first view is the most plausible.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَذِنَتْ لِرَبِّها وَحُقَّتْ...** — "and gives ear to its Lord as it should" [84:5]. The feminine pronouns refer to the earth, just as they referred to the sky in the preceding similar verse. The meaning of the verse has already been explained.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ كَدْحاً فَمُلاقِيهِ...** — "O man! You are laboring toward your Lord with a [constant] laboring, and you will meet Him." [84:6]. Al-Raghib said: "Laboring (الكدح) is striving and toil." Thus, it contains the meaning of a journey. It has also been said: "Laboring is the soul's exertion in work until it leaves an impression upon it." Based on this, it implies the meaning of a journey (السير) as evidenced by its being made transitive with the preposition "toward" (إلى). In any case, the meaning of a journey is present in the word "laboring."

His saying: **فَمُلاقِيهِ...** — "and you will meet Him" is coordinated with "laboring" (كادِحٌ). Through this, He has clarified that the ultimate goal of this journey, striving, and toil is Allah, the Glorified, in His capacity as Lord (الربوبية). That is to say, since man is a servant (عبد) who is sustained (مربوب), owned, and governed, he is striving toward Allah, the Glorified, in His capacity as his Lord, Owner, and the Governor of his affairs. For the servant possesses neither will nor action for himself; rather, he must only will and act according to what his Lord and Master wills and commands. Thus, he is responsible for his will and his action.

From here, several points become clear: First, His saying: **إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ...** — "You are laboring toward your Lord..." contains an argument for the Resurrection (المعاد). This is because lordship (الربوبية) is not complete without servanthood (العبودية), and servanthood is not complete without responsibility (المسئولية), and responsibility is not complete without a return and an accounting (الحساب) for deeds, and an accounting is not complete without requital (الجزاء).

Second: What is meant by "meeting Him" (ملاقاته) is his arrival at a point where there is no command except His command, without any veil (حاجب) obscuring him from his Lord.

Third: The one addressed in the verse is man in his capacity as a human being; thus, it refers to the genus. This is because lordship is universal to every human being.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ...** — "As for him who is given his record in his right hand" [84:7]. This is a detailed exposition following from what was alluded to in His saying: **إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ...** — "You are laboring toward your Lord..."—namely, that there is a return, an inquiry into deeds, and an accounting. What is meant by "the record" (الكتاب) is the scroll of deeds (صحيفة الأعمال), as evidenced by the mention of the accounting. The discussion regarding the meaning of being given the record in the right hand has already preceded in Surah al-Isra and Surah al-Haqqah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً...** — "he will soon receive an easy reckoning" [84:8]. An easy reckoning (الحساب اليسير) is one that is handled with leniency and is free from rigorous scrutiny.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَنْقَلِبُ إِلى أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُوراً...** — "and return to his folks joyfully" [84:9]. What is meant by "folks" (الأهل) are those whom Allah has prepared for him in Paradise, such as the houris (الحور), the youths (الغلمان), and others; this is what the context suggests. It has been said that it refers to his believing kinsfolk who enter Paradise. It has also been said it refers to the group of believers even if they are not his kin, for the believers are brothers. Both views are not without some far-fetchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ وَراءَ ظَهْرِهِ...** — "But as for him who is given his record from behind his back" [84:10]. The adverbial phrase is in the accusative by the removal of the preposition, the implied meaning being "from behind his back." Perhaps they are given their records from behind their backs because their faces will be turned toward their rears, as the Exalted said: **مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَطْمِسَ وُجُوهاً فَنَرُدَّها عَلى أَدْبارِها...** — "before We blot out the faces and turn them backwards..." [4:47].

There is no contradiction between being given their record from behind their backs and being given it in their left hands, as occurs in His saying: **وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِشِمالِهِ...** — "But as for him who is given his record in his left hand..." [69:25]. What has been narrated in the traditions regarding the meaning of giving the record from behind the back will follow in the upcoming discussion on traditions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَسَوْفَ يَدْعُوا ثُبُوراً...** — "he will soon call out for destruction" [84:11]. Destruction (الثبور) is like "woe" (الويل), meaning ruin. Their calling out for destruction is their saying: "O, my ruin!"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَصْلى سَعِيراً...** — "and enter the Blaze" [84:12]. That is, he shall enter a kindled fire whose punishment cannot be described, or he shall endure its heat.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ كانَ فِي أَهْلِهِ مَسْرُوراً...** — "Indeed he used to be joyful among his folks" [84:13]. He was gladdened by what he attained of the worldly enjoyments, and his soul was attracted to its adornment, which made him forget the matter of the Hereafter. The Exalted has censured man's rejoicing in what he attains of worldly good and called it rejoicing without right. The Exalted said, after mentioning the Fire and its punishment: **ذلِكُمْ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَفْرَحُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ بِغَيْرِ الْحَقِّ...** — "That is because you used to rejoice on the earth unduly..." [40:75].

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ ظَنَّ أَنْ لَنْ يَحُورَ...** — "Indeed he thought he would never return" [84:14]. That is, he would never return; what is meant is the return to his Lord for accounting and requital. There was no cause for this [thought] except their deep immersion in sins and offenses that distract from the Hereafter and lead to deeming the Resurrection improbable.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلى إِنَّ رَبَّهُ كانَ بِهِ بَصِيراً...** — "Yes indeed! His Lord was watchful of him" [84:15]. This is a rebuttal of his thought; that is, the matter is not as he thought, but rather he shall return. His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّهُ كانَ بِهِ بَصِيراً...** — "His Lord was watchful of him" is the justification for the aforementioned rebuttal. For Allah, the Glorified, was his Lord, his Owner, and the Governor of his affairs; He encompassed him with knowledge and saw his deeds. He had charged him with what He charged him, and for his deeds there is a requital, whether good or evil. Thus, he must return to Him to be requited according to what he deserves by his deeds.

By this, it becomes clear that His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّهُ كانَ بِهِ بَصِيراً...** — "His Lord was watchful of him" is the presentation of an argument for the necessity of the Resurrection, similar to what preceded in His saying: **إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ إِلى رَبِّكَ...** — "You are laboring toward your Lord..."

It also appears from the sum of these nine verses that the giving of the records and the spreading of the scrolls occur before the accounting, as is also indicated by His saying: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ...** — "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck..." [17:13], which conveys a general meaning.

Furthermore, as you can see, the verses restrict the giving of the record from behind the back to the disbelievers. Thus, the question arises regarding the disobedient believers from among the perpetrators of major sins—those who enter the Fire and remain there for a period, then exit through intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — as stated in the reports from the paths of both sects. These people are not given their records from behind their backs, as that is specific to the disbelievers, nor are they given them in their right hands, for the verses clearly show that the "companions of the right" (أصحاب اليمين) receive an easy reckoning and enter Paradise. There is no way to say they are not given a record at all, due to His saying: **وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ...** — "We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck..." [17:13], which denotes universality.

Some have resolved this difficulty by suggesting they are given their records in their right hands after exiting the Fire.

However, the apparent meaning of the verses—if not their explicit text—is that entering the Fire or Paradise is a consequence resulting from the judgment, which results from the accounting, which results from the giving of the records and the spreading of the scrolls. Thus, there is no meaning to giving the record after exiting the Fire.

Others have speculated that they might be given their records in their left hands, while the giving from behind the back is specific to the disbelievers, as the verses suggest.

Yet, the verses that mention giving the record in the left hand—which are in Surah al-Waqi'ah and Surah al-Haqqah, and similarly in Surah al-Isra—restrict giving the record in the left hand to the disbelievers. It appears from the sum of the verses that those who are given their record in their left hand are the same as those who are given it from behind their backs.

Some have said it is possible they are given their records from behind their backs, and that His saying: **فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً...** — "he will soon receive an easy reckoning" is a case of describing the whole by the attribute of some of its parts.

However, the context does not allow for this trope (المجاز), for the context is one of distinguishing the felicitous from the wretched and identifying each by his specific requital. Thus, there is no justification for merging a group from the people of punishment with the people of Paradise.

Moreover, His saying: **فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ...** — "he will soon receive..." etc., is a beautiful divine promise, and there is no meaning in it encompassing those who do not deserve it, even in the outward sense of the speech.

Yes, it could be said that "ease" (اليسر) and "difficulty" (العسر) are relative terms. The accounting of the disobedient from among the people of faith is "easy" in relation to the accounting of the disbelievers who abide eternally in the Fire, even if it is "difficult" in relation to the accounting of the God-conscious (المتقين).

It could also be said that the division of the people of the gathering into "companions of the right" and "companions of the left" is not exhaustive, as indicated by His saying: **وَكُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً...** — "and you will be three groups..." [56:7]. Thus, the implication of the verses is the exclusion of the "foremost" (المقربين) from both groups, and likewise the "oppressed/weakened" (المستضعفون), as might be understood from His saying: **وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللهِ...** — "And [there are] others waiting for Allah’s decree..." [9:106].

Therefore, it is possible that the division of the people of the gathering into "companions of the right" and "companions of the left" is not an exhaustive division for all of them, but rather a specific mention of the people of Paradise from among the God-conscious and the people of eternal Fire, by describing them as being given the record in the right and the left, for the purpose of calling toward faith and godwariness (التقوى). Similar to this is what is found in Surah al-Mursalat regarding the mention of the Day of Decision, followed by the description of the state of the God-conscious and the deniers only, while people are not restricted to these two groups. Similar examples are found in Surah al-Naba, al-Nazi'at, 'Abasa, al-Infitar, al-Mutaffifin, and others. The purpose in these is to mention a model of the people of faith and obedience and the people of disbelief and denial, while remaining silent about others, so that it may be remembered that felicity lies on the side of godwariness and wretchedness lies on the side of rebellion and tyranny.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِالشَّفَقِ...** — "I swear by the twilight" [84:16]. The twilight (الشفق) is the redness, then the yellowness, then the whiteness that occurs in the west at the beginning of the night.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاللَّيْلِ وَما وَسَقَ...** — "and by the night and what it gathers" [84:17]. That is, what it encloses and collects of what was scattered and spread during the day, such as humans and animals. For they naturally scatter and spread during the day and return to their shelters at night to rest.

Some have interpreted "gathers" (وسق) as "drives," meaning it drives the stars from concealment into appearance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالْقَمَرِ إِذَا اتَّسَقَ...** — "and by the moon when it becomes full" [84:18]. That is, when its light is collected and joined together until it becomes complete and becomes a full moon.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقاً عَنْ طَبَقٍ...** — "you shall surely pass from state to state" [84:19]. This is the response to the oath, and the address is to the people. A "state" (الطبق) is a thing or condition that matches another, whether one is above the other or not. What is meant by it, in any case, is stage after stage that man traverses in his laboring toward his Lord: from the worldly life, then death, then the life of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — then the transition to the Hereafter, then the afterlife, then the accounting and requital.

In this swearing—as you can see—is a confirmation of what was in His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّكَ كادِحٌ...** — "O man! You are laboring..." and what follows it of the news of the Resurrection. It serves as a preamble and preparation for the wonder and reproach in His saying: **فَما لَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ...** — "So what is the matter with them that they do not have faith?" [84:20], and for the warning and glad tidings in His saying: **فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ...** — "so inform them of a painful punishment" [84:21].

The verse contains an indication that the stages man traverses in his journey toward his Lord are ordered and successive.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَما لَهُمْ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ وَإِذا قُرِئَ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقُرْآنُ لا يَسْجُدُونَ...** — "So what is the matter with them that they do not have faith, and when the Quran is recited to them they do not prostrate?" [84:20-21]. The interrogation is for wonder and reproach. Therefore, the transition (الالتفات) from direct address to the third person is appropriate; it is as if when He saw that they do not take heed by His reminder nor accept His exhortation, He turned away from them toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and addressed him saying: "So what is the matter with them that they do not have faith..." etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا يُكَذِّبُونَ وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُوعُونَ...** — "Rather the faithless deny, and Allah knows best what they keep to themselves" [84:22-23]. "They deny" (يُكَذِّبُونَ) implies continuity. Describing them as "those who disbelieve" (الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا) is to indicate the cause of the denial. "Keeping to themselves" (الإيعاء), as has been said, is to place something in a vessel.

The meaning is: they did not abandon faith due to a deficiency in the explanation or a lack of proof, but rather they followed their ancestors and leaders, so they became rooted in disbelief and continued in denial. Allah knows what they have gathered in their chests and concealed in their hearts of disbelief and polytheism.

It has been said that the meaning of His saying: **وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما يُوعُونَ...** — "and Allah knows best what they keep to themselves" is that behind their denial, they have hidden things in their hearts that words cannot encompass and none knows but Allah. This is far-fetched given the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَبَشِّرْهُمْ بِعَذابٍ أَلِيمٍ...** — "so inform them of a painful punishment" [84:24]. Expressing the news of punishment as "glad tidings" (التبشير) is based on sarcasm (التهكم). The sentence is a consequence of the denial.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ...** — "except those who have faith and do righteous deeds: there will be an undiminished reward for them" [84:25]. This is a disconnected exception from the pronoun in "inform them." The meaning of their reward being "undiminished" (غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ) is that it is free from any speech that would weigh heavily upon the recipient.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ...** — "When the sky is split open," he said: "On the Day of Resurrection."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from 'Ali that he said: "The sky will split from the Milky Way."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying: **وَإِذَا الْأَرْضُ مُدَّتْ وَأَلْقَتْ ما فِيها وَتَخَلَّتْ...** — "and when the earth is leveled and throws out what is in it and empties itself," he said: "The earth will be stretched and then split, and the people will come out from it."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated with a good chain from Jabir, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "The earth will be stretched on the Day of Resurrection like the stretching of leather—then the son of Adam will have no place on it except for the space of his two feet."

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from 'Ali عليه‌السلام in a tradition, he said: "The people on that day will be of different descriptions and ranks—among them are those who will be reckoned with an easy reckoning and return to their folks joyfully; among them are those who enter Paradise without an accounting—because they did not linger in any matter of the world—for the accounting there is only for those who were clothed with it here; and among them are those who will be reckoned for the smallest speck (النقير) and the thin skin of a date-stone (القطمير)—and will end up in the punishment of the Blaze."

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain from Ibn Sinan, from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Everyone who is reckoned is punished.' Someone said to him: 'O Messenger of Allah, then where is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَسَوْفَ يُحاسَبُ حِساباً يَسِيراً...** — "he will soon receive an easy reckoning"?' He replied: 'That is the presentation (العرض), meaning the review.'"

**I say:** A similar report was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, and others, from 'Aishah.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام regarding His saying: **فَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ...** — "As for him who is given his record in his right hand," he said: "He is Abu Salamah, 'Abdullah ibn 'Abd al-Asad ibn Hilal al-Makhzumi—he was from the Banu Makhzum. **وَأَمَّا مَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ وَراءَ ظَهْرِهِ...** — 'But as for him who is given his record from behind his back,' he is his brother, al-Aswad ibn 'Abd al-Asad al-Makhzumi—Hamzah ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib killed him on the day of Badr."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لَتَرْكَبُنَّ طَبَقاً عَنْ طَبَقٍ...** — "you shall surely pass from state to state," it was said: "It means hardship after hardship—life, then death, then resurrection, then requital." This was narrated as a *marfu'* tradition.

From *Jawami' al-Jami'*, regarding the verse, from Abu 'Ubaydah: "You shall surely follow the ways (سنن) of those who were before you from the ancients and their conditions." This was narrated from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام.
